metadata.title: Quick-fire: At the edge of the circle
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Fourth in-group/out-group quick-fire. Prior rounds varied social-distance gradients (qf-001), kinds of community (qf-003), and overlapping circles (qf-005). This round focuses on *membership transitions* — moments where the user's own membership, or someone else's, is in flux: choosing sides during community schism, considering leaving, advocating for or against exclusion, vouching for entry, claiming insider status one doesn't quite have.
metadata.domain_anchor: Brewer optimal-distinctiveness; Tajfel social identity theory; Hogg uncertainty-identity theory; sociology of community schism (e.g. Hirschman's Exit, Voice, and Loyalty 1970).

items.prompt: A community you've been part of for years is asking members to publicly take a position on an issue dividing it. You agree privately with one side but going public exposes you to friction with members on the other side.

options.text: Take the position publicly. Standing somewhere matters; the friction is the cost.
Stay neutral publicly; engage privately with people on whichever side you're closer to.

items.prompt: You're considering leaving a long-time group / community / friend circle because it no longer fits. You can leave quietly or you can name what's changed.

options.text: Name what's changed, even though that's a harder conversation. The group deserves it.
Drift away gradually. The relationship has lifecycles; not every leaving needs naming.

items.prompt: Someone is at the edge of your group — they share most of the markers but not all; they could be welcomed in or remain peripheral. They're waiting to see which.

options.text: Actively welcome them. Introduce them to the inside members; make the membership real.
Stay neutral. The existing structure should decide; you're not the gatekeeper.

items.prompt: A long-time member of your group has done something that's divided opinions — some want them excluded, some want them protected. You're asked to weigh in.

options.text: Argue for accountability. What they did matters even though the relationship is long.
Argue for grace. Long relationships have weight; people grow when given the chance.

items.prompt: At a public event, you notice someone presenting themselves as part of a group you're in (an alumni community, professional society, etc.). They're not really — they have a thin or honorary affiliation at best. It's not harming anyone.

options.text: Gently correct in a side conversation. Group membership has meaning; thinly-claimed insider status erodes it.
Let it pass. The boundaries you're enforcing aren't worth the social cost in this moment.

items.prompt: Someone asks you to vouch for them as they apply for membership in a community you're part of (board, society, club, exclusive forum). You have reservations about their fit.

options.text: Decline to vouch, honestly: 'I don't think I'm the right person for this — I have reservations about the fit.'
Vouch with a measured letter. Your reservations don't disqualify them; the group can assess once they're in.
